title: Journey To The Dead
content:
آ آ آ آ Forth from the East, up the ascent of Heaven,
آ آ آ آ Day drove his courser with the Shining Mane;
آ آ آ آ And in Valhalla, from his gable perch,
آ آ آ آ The golden-crested Cock began to crow:
آ آ آ آ Hereafter, in the blackest dead of night,
آ آ آ آ With shrill and dismal cries that Bird shall crow,
آ آ آ آ Warning the Gods that foes draw nigh to Heaven;
آ آ آ آ But now he crew at dawn, a cheerful note,
آ آ آ آ To wake the Gods and Heroes to their tasks.
آ آ آ آ And all the Gods, and all the Heroes, woke.
آ آ آ آ And from their beds the Heroes rose, and donnï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Their arms, and led their horses from the stall,
آ آ آ آ And mounted them, and in Valhallaï؟½s court
آ آ آ آ Were rangï؟½d; and then the daily fray began.
آ آ آ آ And all day long they there are hackï؟½d and hewn
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Mid dust, and groans, and limbs loppï؟½d off, and blood;
آ آ آ آ But all at night return to Odinï؟½s hall
آ آ آ آ Woundless and fresh: such lot is theirs in Heaven.
آ آ آ آ And the Valkyries on their steeds went forth
آ آ آ آ Toward Earth and fights of men; and at their side
آ آ آ آ Skulda, the youngest of the Nornies, rode:
آ آ آ آ And over Bifrost, where is Heimdallï؟½s watch,
آ آ آ آ Past Midgard Fortress, down to Earth they came:
آ آ آ آ There through some battle-field, where men fall fast,
آ آ آ آ Their horses fetlock-deep in blood, they ride,
آ آ آ آ And pick the bravest warriors out for death,
آ آ آ آ Whom they bring back with them at night to Heaven,
آ آ آ آ To glad the Gods, and feast in Odinï؟½s hall.

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ But the Gods went not now, as otherwhile,
آ آ آ آ Into the Tilt-Yard, where the Heroes fought,
آ آ آ آ To feast their eyes with looking on the fray:
آ آ آ آ Nor did they to their Judgement-Place repair
آ آ آ آ By the ash Igdrasil, in Idaï؟½s plain,
آ آ آ آ Where they hold council, and give laws for men:
آ آ آ آ But they went, Odin first, the rest behind,
آ آ آ آ To the hall Gladheim, which is built of gold;
آ آ آ آ Where are in circle rangï؟½d twelve golden chairs,
آ آ آ آ And in the midst one higher, Odinï؟½s throne:
آ آ آ آ There all the Gods in silence sate them down;
آ آ آ آ And thus the Father of the Ages spake:

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ Go quickly, Gods, bring wood to the seashore,
آ آ آ آ With all, which it beseems the dead to have
آ آ آ آ And make a funeral pile on Balderï؟½s ship.
آ آ آ آ On the twelfth day the Gods shall burn his corpse.
آ آ آ آ But Hermod, thou, take Sleipner, and ride down
آ آ آ آ To Helaï؟½s kingdom, to ask Balder back.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ So said he; and the Gods arose, and took
آ آ آ آ Axes and ropes, and at their head came Thor,
آ آ آ آ Shouldering his Hammer, which the Giants know:
آ آ آ آ Forth wended they, and drove their steeds before:
آ آ آ آ And up the dewy mountain tracks they farï؟½d
آ آ آ آ To the dark forests, in the early dawn;
آ آ آ آ And up and down and side and slant they roamï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ And from the glens all day an echo came
آ آ آ آ Of crashing falls; for with his hammer Thor
آ آ آ آ Smote ï؟½mid the rocks the lichen-bearded pines
آ آ آ آ And burst their roots; while to their tops the Gods
آ آ آ آ Made fast the woven ropes, and halï؟½d them down,
آ آ آ آ And loppï؟½d their boughs, and clove them on the sward,
آ آ آ آ And bound the logs behind their steeds to draw,
آ آ آ آ And drove them homeward; and the snorting steeds
آ آ آ آ Went straining through the crackling brushwood down,
آ آ آ آ And by the darkling forest paths the Gods
آ آ آ آ Followï؟½d, and on their shoulders carried boughs.
آ آ آ آ And they came out upon the plain, and passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Asgard, and led their horses to the beach,
آ آ آ آ And loosï؟½d them of their loads on the seashore,
آ آ آ آ And rangï؟½d the wood in stacks by Balderï؟½s ship;
آ آ آ آ And every God went home to his own house.

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ But when the Gods were to the forest gone
آ آ آ آ Hermod led Sleipner from Valhalla forth
آ آ آ آ And saddled him; before that, Sleipner brookï؟½d
آ آ آ آ No meaner hand than Odinï؟½s on his mane,
آ آ آ آ On his broad back no lesser rider bore:
آ آ آ آ Yet docile now he stood at Hermodï؟½s side,
آ آ آ آ Arching his neck, and glad to be bestrode,
آ آ آ آ Knowing the God they went to seek, how dear.
آ آ آ آ But Hermod mounted him, and sadly farï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ In silence, up the dark untravellï؟½d road
آ آ آ آ Which branches from the north of Heaven, and went
آ آ آ آ All day; and Daylight wanï؟½d, and Night came on.
آ آ آ آ And all that night he rode, and journeyï؟½d so,
آ آ آ آ Nine days, nine nights, towards the northern ice,
آ آ آ آ Through valleys deep-engulphï؟½d, by roaring streams:
آ آ آ آ And on the tenth morn ho beheld the bridge
آ آ آ آ Which spans with golden arches Giallï؟½s stream,
آ آ آ آ And on the bridge a Damsel watching armï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ In the strait passage, at the further end,
آ آ آ آ Where the road issues between walling rocks.
آ آ آ آ Scant space that Warder left for passers by;
آ آ آ آ But, as when cowherds in October drive
آ آ آ آ Their kine across a snowy mountain pass
آ آ آ آ To winter pasture on the southern side,
آ آ آ آ And on the ridge a wagon chokes the way,
آ آ آ آ Wedgï؟½d in the snow; then painfully the hinds
آ آ آ آ With goad and shouting urge their cattle past,
آ آ آ آ Plunging through deep untrodden banks of snow
آ آ آ آ To right and left, and warm steam fills the air
آ آ آ آ So on the bridge that Damsel blockï؟½d the way,
آ آ آ آ And questionï؟½d Hermod as he came, and said:

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ ï؟½Who art thou on thy black and fiery horse
آ آ آ آ Under whose hoofs the bridge oï؟½er Giallï؟½s stream
آ آ آ آ Rumbles and shakes? Tell me thy race and home.
آ آ آ آ But yestermorn five troops of dead passï؟½d by
آ آ آ آ Bound on their way below to Helaï؟½s realm,
آ آ آ آ Nor shook the bridge so much as thou alone.
آ آ آ آ And thou hast flesh and colour on thy cheeks
آ آ آ آ Like men who live and draw the vital air;
آ آ آ آ Nor lookï؟½st thou pale and wan, like men deceasï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Souls bound below, my daily passers here.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And the fleet-footed Hermod answerï؟½d her:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½O Damsel, Hermod am I callï؟½d, the son
آ آ آ آ Of Odin; and my high-roofï؟½d house is built
آ آ آ آ Far hence, in Asgard, in the City of Gods:
آ آ آ آ And Sleipner, Odinï؟½s horse, is this I ride.
آ آ آ آ And I come, sent this road on Balderï؟½s track:
آ آ آ آ Say then, if he hath crossï؟½d thy bridge or no?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He spake; the Warder of the bridge replied:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½O Hermod, rarely do the feet of Gods
آ آ آ آ Or of the horses of the Gods resound
آ آ آ آ Upon my bridge; and, when they cross, I know.
آ آ آ آ Balder hath gone this way, and taï؟½en the road
آ آ آ آ Below there, to the north, toward Helaï؟½s realm.
آ آ آ آ From here the cold white mist can be discernï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ Not lit with sun, but through the darksome air
آ آ آ آ By the dim vapour-blotted light of stars,
آ آ آ آ Which hangs over the ice where lies the road.
آ آ آ آ For in that ice are lost those northern streams
آ آ آ آ Freezing and ridging in their onward flow,
آ آ آ آ Which from the fountain of Vergelmer run,
آ آ آ آ The spring that bubbles up by Helaï؟½s throne.
آ آ آ آ There are the joyless seats, the haunt of ghosts,
آ آ آ آ Helaï؟½s pale swarms; and there was Balder bound.
آ آ آ آ Ride on; pass free: but he by this is there.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ She spake, and steppï؟½d aside, and left him room.
آ آ آ آ And Hermod greeted her, and gallopï؟½d by
آ آ آ آ Across the bridge; then she took post again.
آ آ آ آ But northward Hermod rode, the way below:
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er a darksome tract, which knows no sun,
آ آ آ آ But by the blotted light of stars, he farï؟½d;
آ آ آ آ And he came down to Oceanï؟½s northern strand
آ آ آ آ At the drear ice, beyond the Giantsï؟½ home:
آ آ آ آ Thence on he journeyï؟½d oï؟½er the fields of ice
آ آ آ آ Still north, until he met a stretching wall
آ آ آ آ Barring his way, and in the wall a grate.
آ آ آ آ Then he dismounted, and drew tight the girths,
آ آ آ آ On the smooth ice, of Sleipner, Odinï؟½s horse,
آ آ آ آ And made him leap the grate, and came within.
آ آ آ آ And he beheld spread round him Helaï؟½s realm,
آ آ آ آ The plains of Niflheim, where dwell the dead,
آ آ آ آ And heard the thunder of the streams of Hell.
آ آ آ آ For near the wall the river of Roaring flows,
آ آ آ آ Outmost: the others near the centre run
آ آ آ آ The Storm, the Abyss, the Howling, and the Pain:
آ آ آ آ Those flow by Helaï؟½s throne, and near their spring.
آ آ آ آ And from the dark flockï؟½d up the shadowy tribes:
آ آ آ آ And as the swallows crowd the bulrush-beds
آ آ آ آ Of some clear river, issuing from a lake,
آ آ آ آ On autumn days, before they cross the sea;
آ آ آ آ And to each bulrush-crest a swallow hangs
آ آ آ آ Swinging, and others skim the river streams,
آ آ آ آ And their quick twittering fills the banks and shores
آ آ آ آ So around Hermod swarmï؟½d the twittering ghosts.
آ آ آ آ Women, and infants, and young men who died
آ آ آ آ Too soon for fame, with white ungraven shields;
آ آ آ آ And old men, known to Glory, but their star
آ آ آ آ Betrayï؟½d them, and of wasting age they died,
آ آ آ آ Not wounds: yet, dying, they their armour wore,
آ آ آ آ And now have chief regard in Helaï؟½s realm.
آ آ آ آ Behind flockï؟½d wrangling up a piteous crew,
آ آ آ آ Greeted of none, disfeaturï؟½d and forlorn
آ آ آ آ Cowards, who were in sloughs interrï؟½d alive:
آ آ آ آ And round them still the wattled hurdles hung

آ آ آ آ Wherewith they stampï؟½d them down, and trod them deep,
آ آ آ آ To hide their shameful memory from men.
آ آ آ آ But all he passï؟½d unhailï؟½d, and reachï؟½d the throne
آ آ آ آ Of Hela, and saw, near it, Balder crownï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And Hela sat thereon, with countenance stern;
آ آ آ آ And thus bespake him first the solemn Queen:

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ ï؟½Unhappy, how hast thou endurï؟½d to leave
آ آ آ آ The light, and journey to the cheerless land
آ آ آ آ Where idly flit about the feeble shades
آ آ آ آ How didst thou cross the bridge oï؟½er Giallï؟½s stream,
آ آ آ آ Being alive, and come to Oceanï؟½s shore?
آ آ آ آ Or how oï؟½erleap the grate that bars the wall?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ She spake: but down off Sleipner Hermod sprang,
آ آ آ آ And fell before her feet, and claspï؟½d her knees;
آ آ آ آ And spake, and mild entreated her, and said:

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ ï؟½O Hela, wherefore should the Gods declare
آ آ آ آ Their errands to each other, or the ways
آ آ آ آ They go? the errand and the way is known.
آ آ آ آ Thou knowï؟½st, thou knowï؟½st, what grief we have in Heaven
آ آ آ آ For Balder, whom thou holdï؟½st by right below:
آ آ آ آ Restore him, for what part fulfils he here?
آ آ آ آ Shall he shed cheer over the cheerless seats,
آ آ آ آ And touch the apathetic ghosts with joy?
آ آ آ آ Not for such end, O Queen, thou holdï؟½st thy realm.
آ آ آ آ For Heaven was Balder born, the City of Gods
آ آ آ آ And Heroes, where they live in light and joy:
آ آ آ آ Thither restore him, for his place is there.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He spoke; and grave replied the solemn Queen:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hermod, for he thou art, thou Son of Heaven!
آ آ آ آ A strange unlikely errand, sure, is thine.
آ آ آ آ Do the Gods send to me to make them blest?
آ آ آ آ Small bliss my race hath of the Gods obtainï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Three mighty children to my Father Lok
آ آ آ آ Did Angerbode, the Giantess, bring forth
آ آ آ آ Fenris the Wolf, the Serpent huge, and Me:
آ آ آ آ Of these the Serpent in the sea ye cast,
آ آ آ آ Who since in your despite hath waxï؟½d amain,
آ آ آ آ And now with gleaming ring enfolds the world:
آ آ آ آ Me on this cheerless nether world ye threw
آ آ آ آ And gave me nine unlighted realms to rule:
آ آ آ آ While on his island in the lake, afar,
آ آ آ آ Made fast to the borï؟½d crag, by wile not strength
آ آ آ آ Subduï؟½d, with limber chains lives Fenris bound.
آ آ آ آ Lok still subsists in Heaven, our Father wise,
آ آ آ آ Your mate, though loathï؟½d, and feasts in Odinï؟½s hall;
آ آ آ آ But him too foes await, and netted snares,
آ آ آ آ And in a cave a bed of needle rocks,
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er his visage serpents dropping gall.
آ آ آ آ Yet he shall one day rise, and burst his bonds,
آ آ آ آ And with himself set us his offspring free,
آ آ آ آ When he guides Muspelï؟½s children to their bourne.
آ آ آ آ Till then in peril or in pain we live,
آ آ آ آ Wrought by the Gods: and ask the Gods our aid?
آ آ آ آ Howbeit we abide our day: till then,
آ آ آ آ We do not as some feebler haters do,
آ آ آ آ Seek to afflict our foes with petty pangs,
آ آ آ آ Helpless to better us, or ruin them.
آ آ آ آ Come then; if Balder was so dear belovï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And this is true, and such a loss is Heavenï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Hear, how to Heaven may Balder be restorï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Show me through all the world the signs of grief:
آ آ آ آ Fails but one thing to grieve, here Balder stops:
آ آ آ آ Let all that lives and moves upon the earth
آ آ آ آ Weep him, and all that is without life weep:
آ آ آ آ Let Gods, men, brutes, beweep him; plants and stones.
آ آ آ آ So shall I know the lost was dear indeed,
آ آ آ آ And bend my heart, and give him back to Heaven.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ She spake; and Hermod answerï؟½d her, and said:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hela, such as thou sayï؟½st, the terms shall be.
آ آ آ آ But come, declare me this, and truly tell:
آ آ آ آ May I, ere I depart, bid Balder hail
آ آ آ آ Or is it here withheld to greet the dead?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He spake; and straightway Hela answerï؟½d him:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hermod, greet Balder if thou wilt, and hold
آ آ آ آ Converse: his speech remains, though he he dead.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ And straight to Balder Hermod turnï؟½d, and spake:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Even in the abode of Death, O Balder, hail!
آ آ آ آ Thou hearï؟½st, if hearing, like as speech, is thine,
آ آ آ آ The terms of thy releasement hence to Heaven:
آ آ آ آ Fear nothing but that all shall be fulfillï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ For not unmindful of thee are the Gods
آ آ آ آ Who see the light, and blest in Asgard dwell;
آ آ آ آ Even here they seek thee out, in Helaï؟½s realm.
آ آ آ آ And sure of all the happiest far art thou
آ آ آ آ Who ever have been known in Earth or Heaven:
آ آ آ آ Alive, thou wert of Gods the most belovï؟½d:
آ آ آ آ And now thou sittest crownï؟½d by Helaï؟½s side,
آ آ آ آ Here, and hast honour among all the dead.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He spake; and Balder utterï؟½d him reply,
آ آ آ آ But feebly, as a voice far off; he said:

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ ï؟½Hermod the nimble, gild me not my death.
آ آ آ آ Better to live a slave, a capturï؟½d man,
آ آ آ آ Who scatters rushes in a masterï؟½s ball,
آ آ آ آ Than be a crownï؟½d king here, and rule the dead.
آ آ آ آ And now I count not of these terms as safe
آ آ آ آ To be fulfillï؟½d, nor my return as sure,
آ آ آ آ Though I be lovï؟½d, and many mourn my death:
آ آ آ آ For double-minded ever was the seed
آ آ آ آ Of Lok, and double are the gifts they give.
آ آ آ آ Howbeit, report thy message; and therewith,
آ آ آ آ To Odin, to my Father, take this ring,
آ آ آ آ Memorial of me, whether savï؟½d or no:
آ آ آ آ And tell the Heaven-born Gods how thou hast seen
آ آ آ آ Me sitting here below by Hela s side,
آ آ آ آ Crownï؟½d, having honour among all the dead.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ آ He spake, and raisï؟½d his hand, and gave the ring.
آ آ آ آ And with inscrutable regard the Queen
آ آ آ آ Of Hell beheld them, and the ghosts stood dumb.
آ آ آ آ But Hermod took the ring, and yet once more
آ آ آ آ Kneelï؟½d and did homage to the solemn Queen;
آ آ آ آ Then mounted Sleipner, and set forth to ride
آ آ آ آ Back, through the astonishï؟½d tribes of dead, to Heaven.
آ آ آ آ And to the wall he came, and found the grate
آ آ آ آ Lifted, and issued on the fields of ice;
آ آ آ آ And oï؟½er the ice he farï؟½d to Oceanï؟½s strand,
آ آ آ آ And up from thence, a wet and misty road,
آ آ آ آ To the armï؟½d Damselï؟½s bridge, and Giallï؟½s stream.
آ آ آ آ Worse was that way to go than to return,
آ آ آ آ For him: for others all return is barrï؟½d.
آ آ آ آ Nine days he took to go, two to return;
آ آ آ آ And on the twelfth morn saw the light of Heaven.
آ آ آ آ And as a traveller in the early dawn
آ آ آ آ To the steep edge of some great valley comes
آ آ آ آ Through which a river flows, and sees beneath
آ آ آ آ Clouds of white rolling vapours fill the vale,
آ آ آ آ But oï؟½er them, on the farther slope, descries
آ آ آ آ Vineyards, and crofts, and pastures, bright with sun
آ آ آ آ So Hermod, oï؟½er the fog between, saw Heaven.
آ آ آ آ And Sleipner snorted, for he smelt the air
آ آ آ آ Of Heaven: and mightily, as wingï؟½d, he flew.
آ آ آ آ And Hermod saw the towers of Asgard rise:
آ آ آ آ And he drew near, and heard no living voice
آ آ آ آ In Asgard; and the golden halls were dumb.
آ آ آ آ Then Hermod knew what labour held the Gods:
آ آ آ آ And through the empty streets he rode, and passï؟½d
آ آ آ آ Under the gate-house to the sands, and found
آ آ آ آ The Gods on the seashore by Balderï؟½s ship.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Matthew Arnold represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Matthew Arnold's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
